content.problemStatement: Given a insertionSort function that accepts an array as a parameter, sort the array using the **Insertion sort** algorithm.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains an integer N, the number of elements in the array. The second line contains N space‑separated integers.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line with the N integers of the sorted array in non‑decreasing order, separated by a single space.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The single test case contains 8 numbers. After applying insertion sort the numbers are ordered increasingly.
The array is already sorted; insertion sort leaves it unchanged.
Insertion sort reverses the descending order into ascending order.

solution.approach: Insertion sort builds the sorted array one element at a time. Starting from the second element, we keep the current element (key) and shift all larger elements of the already sorted prefix one position to the right. The correct position for the key is then filled. This algorithm works in‑place, needs only O(1) extra memory and runs in O(N^2) time in the worst case, which is acceptable for N ≤ 1000.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N^2) per test case, O(T·N^2) total.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) extra space besides the input array.
